summary: DHH writes a lighthearted personal essay about rollerblading through Copenhagen streets after seven years with the same pair of skates. He admits that while exercise and sunshine are factors, the real motivation is showing off by outpacing cyclists at traffic lights. He humorously outlines the tactical considerations of showing off, including avoiding competitive cyclists and knowing when to stop before losing steam.
key_points: Rollerblades have superior acceleration compared to bicycles, making it possible to dramatically outpace cyclists from a standing start
Showing off requires tactical awareness: avoid competitive cyclists who might spoil the display, and always have an exit strategy
Maintaining an 'aura of invincibility' means never letting people see you run out of steam — knowing when to fold is essential
The author embraces the shallowness of the impulse rather than pretending it's about exercise or sunshine
money_quotes: I'd love to claim it's all for the good of the exercise. Or enjoying the sun. Well, it's also that. But far more importantly, it's about showing off.
I know, it sounds shallow. And it is.
The rush you get by flying off as the lights turn green, leaving twenty bicycle riders feeling like they aren't moving, is most enjoyable.
Showing off isn't always a sure-fire hit, though. Multiple issues to be aware of.
Accelerating like mad and going the distance is mutually exclusive, hence you need a plan on when to drop out of the race.
Keeping an aura of invincibility at all times is part of the role you assume when choosing to show off.
key_insight: Showing off effectively requires self-awareness, tactical planning, and knowing when to stop — because being caught fading is worse than never having sprinted at all.
